name: Tutorial 1: Part D IRA Drug Exclusion Analysis
description: Identifies non-IRA Medicare Part D drug candidates by importing the CMS spending dataset, loading the IRA negotiated drug reference table, and using a JOIN to filter excluded drugs. Demonstrates the core AW workflow: import, reference JOIN, query, and export.

narration.pre: Starting a new analysis session. We will analyze Medicare Part D drug spending data to identify drugs that are NOT on the IRA negotiated price list — potential candidates for the GUARD payment model.
narration.post: Session initialized. The first step is to import the dataset.

narration.pre: Importing the CMS Medicare Part D Drug Spending dataset. This is a 500-row sample from the full CMS release, containing brand names, generic names, manufacturer info, and spending data across 5 years (2019-2023). Each row represents one drug-manufacturer combination.
narration.post: Dataset loaded: 500 rows, 46 columns. The data includes spending columns (Tot_Spndng), dosage units, claim counts, beneficiary counts, and cost-per-unit metrics for each year. Insights will auto-generate from the column structure.

narration.pre: Before querying, inspect the schema. Click Schema to see every column name and type. Medicare datasets have dense naming conventions — Tot_Spndng, Avg_Spnd_Per_Dsg_Unt_Wghtd — so reviewing the schema first saves time.
narration.post: 46 columns. The spending columns (Tot_Spndng, Avg_Spnd_Per_Clm) are the monetary fields you will query. Brnd_Name and Gnrc_Name identify the drug. Tot_Mftr counts manufacturers — used later to approximate single-source status.

narration.pre: Click Preview to see the first rows of actual data. This confirms the data loaded correctly and gives you a feel for the values — drug names, spending amounts, manufacturer counts.
narration.post: Data looks clean. Notice the dollar amounts in the spending columns — these are formatted as currency. The Brnd_Name column has trailing asterisks on some drugs (e.g. 'Stelara*') — AW handles this automatically with LIKE pattern matching.

narration.pre: AI is analyzing the dataset structure to generate automatic insights. This uses column names and aggregate statistics only — no raw data leaves your machine.
narration.post: 5 insight cards generated. These highlight spending concentration, outliers, and trends in the data. Each card has an Explore in Query button to load the supporting SQL. For now, we move to the IRA exclusion analysis.

narration.pre: Loading the IRA Negotiated Drugs reference table. This contains 35 drugs selected for Medicare price negotiation under the Inflation Reduction Act (Rounds 1-3). We will JOIN this against the spending dataset to identify which drugs are excluded from further analysis.
narration.post: Reference table loaded: 35 drugs with columns drug_name, generic_name, ira_round, effective_date, manufacturer, and indication_category. The table is now available as 'reference' in SQL queries. String columns are title-cased to match CMS naming conventions.

details.question: How many distinct drugs are in the dataset, excluding the 'Overall' manufacturer summary rows?

narration.pre: First question in plain English: 'How many distinct drugs are in the dataset, excluding the Overall manufacturer summary rows?' This gives us the universe of drugs we're working with.
narration.post: 272 distinct brand-name drugs in the dataset. This is our starting universe. The AI correctly filtered out the CMS summary rows and counted distinct brand names. The next step is to see which ones are the biggest spenders.

details.question: What are the top 10 drugs by 2023 spending, excluding the 'Overall' manufacturer rows?

narration.pre: Next: 'What are the top 10 drugs by 2023 spending, excluding the Overall manufacturer rows?' This shows where Medicare Part D dollars are most concentrated.
narration.post: Top 10 spenders identified. Eliquis and Ozempic lead the list with billions in spending. Note that several of these drugs appear on the IRA negotiated list — they'll be filtered out in the next steps.

details.sql: SELECT DISTINCT d.Brnd_Name, d.Gnrc_Name, r.ira_round, r.effective_date FROM dataset d INNER JOIN reference r ON d.Brnd_Name = r.drug_name WHERE d.Mftr_Name != 'Overall' ORDER BY r.ira_round, d.Brnd_Name

narration.pre: Now we JOIN the spending dataset against the IRA reference table. This INNER JOIN matches brand names between the two tables, showing us exactly which drugs in our dataset are on the IRA negotiated list. The reference table was title-cased on import so brand names match without needing LOWER() wrappers.
narration.post: 22 drugs in our dataset match the IRA negotiated list. These span all 3 IRA rounds. This is the exclusion set — these drugs have negotiated prices and should be filtered out when identifying GUARD payment model candidates.

details.sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT d.Brnd_Name) AS non_ira_drugs FROM dataset d LEFT JOIN reference r ON d.Brnd_Name = r.drug_name WHERE d.Mftr_Name != 'Overall' AND r.drug_name IS NULL

narration.pre: Now the key query: how many drugs remain AFTER excluding IRA-negotiated drugs? We use a LEFT JOIN with a NULL check — any drug that doesn't match the reference table is a non-IRA drug. This is the standard exclusion pattern: LEFT JOIN + IS NULL.
narration.post: 202 non-IRA drugs remain after excluding IRA-negotiated brands. These are the potential GUARD payment model candidates — drugs with Medicare spending that are not subject to IRA price negotiation.

details.sql: SELECT d.Brnd_Name, d.Gnrc_Name, d.Tot_Spndng_2023, d.Tot_Benes_2023, d.Avg_Spnd_Per_Bene_2023 FROM dataset d LEFT JOIN reference r ON d.Brnd_Name = r.drug_name WHERE d.Mftr_Name != 'Overall' AND r.drug_name IS NULL ORDER BY d.Tot_Spndng_2023 DESC LIMIT 50

narration.pre: Finally, let's see the top 50 non-IRA drugs by 2023 spending. These are the highest-value candidates for the GUARD payment model — drugs with significant Medicare spending that are not protected by IRA negotiated prices.
narration.post: Top 50 GUARD candidates identified, ranked by 2023 spending. This is the core deliverable of the analysis: a prioritized list of non-IRA drugs that could be subject to the GUARD payment model. The next step would be to export this result and apply additional filters (orphan drug status, biosimilar availability, manufacturer MFN deals).

narration.pre: Exporting the top 50 non-IRA candidates to Excel for further analysis and stakeholder review.
narration.post: Export complete. The Excel file contains the ranked candidate list ready for policy review. In a full analysis, you would apply additional reference table JOINs (orphan drug status, USP therapeutic categories) to further classify these candidates.

narration.pre: The analysis is complete. Generate Summary produces a structured memo of the IRA exclusion methodology — which datasets were used, which filters were applied, and what the candidate list represents. This is the audit trail for the analytical work.
narration.post: The Analysis Summary captures the full workflow: Part D spending data imported, IRA reference table joined, exclusion filter applied, candidate list exported. The four-section memo (Findings, Methodology, Limitations, Open Items) is ready for review or export as Markdown.

narration.pre: Analysis complete. Let's review what we accomplished.
narration.post: Session summary: Started with 272 Part D drugs, identified 33 IRA-negotiated drugs via reference table JOIN, isolated 243 non-IRA candidates, and exported the top 50 by spending. This workflow — import, reference JOIN, exclusion query, export — is the foundation of CMS drug policy analysis in Analytics Workbench.

resume_state.last_sql: SELECT d.Brnd_Name, d.Gnrc_Name, d.Tot_Spndng_2023, d.Tot_Benes_2023, d.Avg_Spnd_Per_Bene_2023 FROM dataset d LEFT JOIN reference r ON d.Brnd_Name = r.drug_name WHERE d.Mftr_Name != 'Overall' AND r.drug_name IS NULL ORDER BY d.Tot_Spndng_2023 DESC LIMIT 50
